title: The Mystery of the Missing Mascot

narrative.intro: The school mascot costume has vanished from the gym storage room — right before the big pep rally! Principal Reeves has called in the Logic Bureau (that's you) to interview witnesses and figure out what happened. But watch out: everyone's got a theory, and not all of them make sense...
narrative.outro: Thanks to your sharp eye for bad reasoning, the costume was found stuffed behind the bleachers — right where the janitor said he saw someone heading. The real story? A student borrowed it for a TikTok and forgot to return it. Case closed, detective.

cases.briefing: Your first interview. Tommy saw something at lunch — but is his logic as solid as he thinks?
cases.speaker: Tommy Chen
claims: I was shooting hoops near the gym at lunch.
I saw Jake come out of the storage room around 12:10.
Everyone knows Jake is a troublemaker, so he obviously took the mascot costume.
The gym door locks automatically after that.
options: Tommy is using Jake's reputation instead of actual evidence that Jake took it.
Tommy didn't actually see Jake holding the costume.
cases.explanation: Tommy jumps from 'Jake has a bad reputation' to 'Jake did it.' But someone's character isn't proof they committed a specific act. This is called Ad Hominem — attacking the person instead of presenting real evidence.

cases.briefing: The class president has a theory too. She seems confident — but is she reasoning correctly?
cases.speaker: Mia Park
claims: I organized the pep rally, so I know exactly who had access.
Only the drama club and the cheer squad had gym keys.
I asked around and literally every single student thinks the drama club did it.
We should just search their lockers.
options: Just because lots of students suspect the drama club doesn't make them guilty.
Mia shouldn't have access to other students' lockers.
cases.explanation: Mia's evidence is 'everyone thinks they did it' — but popular opinion isn't proof! Lots of people believing something doesn't make it true. This is the Bandwagon fallacy — using crowd agreement as if it were evidence.

cases.briefing: Coach Davis is frustrated and wants this solved fast. A little too fast, maybe?
cases.speaker: Coach Davis
claims: Look, I've coached here for 15 years and nothing like this has happened.
The storage room lock wasn't broken, so someone had a key.
Either it was a student who stole a key, or we cancel the pep rally entirely.
Those are the only options I see.
options: Coach is pretending there are only two options when other explanations exist.
The coach has no evidence that a key was stolen.
15 years of experience doesn't make the coach's theory correct.
cases.explanation: Coach Davis presents only two options: stolen key or cancel everything. But there could be other explanations — maybe someone borrowed the costume with permission, maybe a teacher left the door open, maybe there's a spare key. This is a False Dilemma — pretending there are only two choices when more exist.

cases.briefing: A popular student posts a 'theory' online. Social media detective work can be tricky...
cases.speaker: ViralVince
claims: Ok so I just found out the mascot costume is missing.
The drama club had rehearsal yesterday and the costume was still there.
Drama club rehearsal ended, and THEN the costume disappeared.
So obviously rehearsal caused the costume to go missing lol. Case closed.
options: Just because the costume disappeared after rehearsal doesn't mean rehearsal caused it.
Vince doesn't have enough followers to be a reliable source.
The drama club should have locked up after rehearsal.
cases.explanation: Vince assumes that because the costume vanished AFTER drama rehearsal, the rehearsal must have CAUSED it to vanish. But timing isn't proof of cause! Maybe someone else came in later. This is called False Cause — assuming that because B happened after A, A must have caused B.

cases.briefing: The janitor has a new lead. But does one clue really prove the whole theory?
cases.speaker: Mr. Gonzalez
claims: I clean the gym every evening after activities end.
Yesterday I noticed muddy footprints leading toward the bleachers.
Last month, a 5th grader tracked mud into the library and got in trouble for it.
So a 5th grader definitely took the costume and hid it behind the bleachers.
options: Mr. Gonzalez is using one past incident to blame all 5th graders for this.
Muddy footprints don't prove anyone took the costume.
The janitor should have reported the footprints immediately.
cases.explanation: One 5th grader tracked mud once, so Mr. Gonzalez concludes a 5th grader must be responsible this time too. That's like saying 'my neighbor's dog barked last week, so every noise must be a dog.' This is Hasty Generalization — jumping to a big conclusion from too little evidence.

cases.briefing: Final interview. The vice principal has a 'comprehensive' theory — but something's off...
cases.speaker: VP Rodriguez
claims: We've been reviewing the security camera footage from the hallway.
Three students were seen near the gym between 12:00 and 12:30.
I think the real issue here is that our school doesn't have enough funding for proper security cameras inside the gym.
If we had better cameras, this never would have happened.
I'll be presenting a budget proposal to the school board next week.
options: The VP is switching from 'who took the costume' to 'we need more budget' — a completely different topic.
Security cameras wouldn't necessarily prevent theft.
Three students near the gym isn't enough evidence to identify the thief.
The VP should focus on interviewing those three students first.
cases.explanation: VP Rodriguez starts with useful evidence (camera footage, three students), then suddenly pivots to school funding — a totally different issue! The budget has nothing to do with solving this case. This is a Red Herring — introducing an irrelevant topic to distract from the real question.
